content:
Hiya - I was chatting with some nouveau folks, and Nova came to mind when the topic of nouveau and running NVK on the open NVIDIA kernel module came up. This led me to ask the question: how usable is R4L if I wanted to write my own GPU driver for the kernel?

I have an AMD GPU (on a laptop, no less), and I know amdgpu is Fine, but I want to play around with writing a Rust driver for it anyways, starting from getting my laptop display showing something and maybe even stepping up to supporting amdgpu IOCTLS and other interfaces.

Any pointers? Development-wise, I should always have a booting system even if a kernel breaks (NixOS user over here, though only as of recent), but if someone already has something set up to build a linuxPackage in Nix, I would appreciate seeing how to use it for my own purposes.

Add.: I probably wouldn't ever plan on upstreaming it, but it might be good to use as a reference for any future efforts to Rust-ify amdgpu, if one so wanted, so it being super usable isn't a worry. I just want to use it for learning experience, since I'm far more comfortable writing Rust than C, and I'd like to do something that isn't 99% banging my head against a wall.
I would also not touch a mailing list with a ten foot pole, so that immediately disqualifies me upstreaming this theoretical crabified-amdgpu.

Actually, I might first ask HP about some docs on their Probook x360 laptop, and see if they have anything I can use to add hinge rotation detection support. That kinda ticks me off. Not sure if the Windows side supports it though - if it does, I'll definitely ask.

Maybe I could do that as an easier kickstarter? Would still need assistance in setting up a devenv, specifically for Nix.

how usable is R4L if I wanted to write my own GPU driver for the kernel?

It depends on what you mean -- it works, but there are a lot of abstractions missing since they are in the process of upstreaming. I would recommend checking first what others have ready downstream, e.g. Asahi and Nova.

if someone already has something set up to build a linuxPackage in Nix

@Fiona Behrens, @Benno Lossin, @Andreas Hindborg et al. worked on Nix. We have minimal documentation upstream, but it may help: https://docs.kernel.org/rust/quick-start.html#nix. They may able to tell you more. There is also https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/nix (please see the recent PR there).

I would also not touch a mailing list with a ten foot pole

May I ask why? (i.e. it is always useful for us to know reasons why people may or may not contribute etc.)

Miguel Ojeda said:

May I ask why? (i.e. it is always useful for us to know reasons why people may or may not contribute etc.)

It's hard to describe. The general feel of them is just off, and the barrier entry is too high. I don't want to set up my email client to submit some patches when there are projects on other git platforms like GitLab, GitHub, and Codeberg that I can submit to with ease and have it feel more natural to review and communicate on.

Dallas Strouse said:

Miguel Ojeda said:

May I ask why? (i.e. it is always useful for us to know reasons why people may or may not contribute etc.)

It's hard to describe. The general feel of them is just off, and the barrier entry is too high. I don't want to set up my email client to submit some patches when there are projects on other git platforms like GitLab, GitHub, and Codeberg that I can submit to with ease and have it feel more natural to review and communicate on.

While I respect your position on this matter, I am always puzzled when people who are capable of producing software components as complicated as something like a modern GPU driver, do not care to use the two hours it takes to familiarize themselves with the arcane workflow that is the LKML.

Before I started using mailing lists for code contributions, I also had a negative view of mailing lists. But I must say, once I became proficient with the workflow, I far prefer email lists for code review, rather than mousing around in some web GUI to do the same thing. To each his own, I guess.

But it’s actually not that difficult to learn, and it never hurts to learn a bit of new stuff. Also, while the Linux kernel mailing lists have a reputation for being sort of hostile, we strive to be very friendly in the rust subsystem. I understand that GPU would go elsewhere, but the GPU people also seem to be nice. At least the ones that hang out on the rust list (I do not follow DRM in general, so can’t speak for that). TBH I have seen some pretty hostile stuff going down on GitHub as well. Etiquette and culture is probably more tied to the community than the media of contribution.

My dislike of mailing lists is the format itself, not the community. Maybe sometime in the future I'll get over it, but for now I'll just do things outside of official kernel channels and if someone likes something, they can submit it themselves.

Believe me when I say the only reason I'm here talking about it is because there's a Zulip and GitHub available for R4L.

I don't want to set up my email client to submit some patches

Nowadays it is possible to submit via b4. It still requires learning the tool, of course, but at least that removes issues with some email providers and/or clients.

if someone likes something, they can submit it themselves.

Thanks, that could be useful eventually. In any case, if that happens, please remember that contributions still need to be properly signed off etc.

Zulip and GitHub available for R4L.

Glad we chose that then! :) (Just in case: code submissions happen via the mailing list, not PRs in GitHub.)

Thanks for the feedback!

Hi @Dallas,

We seem to have a lot of components falling in place as of recently. Quite a few of them are from the Asahi project and are in the process of being upstreamed, but @Danilo Krummrich et al have made nice progress in this area too.

The main missing piece is job submission and synchronization, but I am sure we will get this sorted out eventually. Power management is also missing in general.
This is a complex topic, so in the interest of not writing a massive wall of text here feel free to ask anything more specific.

writing an amdgpu driver is a very daunting task, the display and power management code in the upstream driver is "imported" from AMD's internal developments, there are some horrendous calculations around display bandwidth and the DC layer does all the display hw mgmt stuff, it would be a fun project, but there are some very seriously large hurdles to clear to get any sort of support past one device
